title: Factory Pattern: The Complete Walkthrough
subtitle: From scattered if-else to clean creation -- an interactive discussion that builds the pattern step by step, through questions, doubts, and aha moments.

sections.title: 10 Services, 10 Copies of the Same if-else

content.value: Imagine you have OrderService, PaymentService, ShippingService, ReturnService, ReportService... all 10 of them need to send notifications. Each one has this exact same code:

content.title: The Problem: Scattered Creation

content.value: Now the PM says "add WhatsApp notifications." You touch 10 files. Constructor of SMSNotification changes? 10 files again. Testing? Every service is tightly coupled to every notification type.
The real problem isn't the if-else itself -- it's that creation logic is SCATTERED across 10 places. One change ripples everywhere.

content.question: We created a Notification interface and concrete implementations (EmailNotification, SMSNotification). We're swapping them based on type. That's the Strategy pattern, right? How are we going towards Factory?
content.answer:
You're right to notice the similarity! Strategy and Factory both use interfaces + concrete implementations. But they solve different problems:

Strategy = WHICH algorithm to RUN on an existing object (behavior swapping)
Factory = WHICH object to CREATE from scratch (creation selection)

Strategy gave us the interface and the concrete classes. But WHO decides which concrete class to instantiate? That selection logic -- the if-else that picks 'new EmailNotification()' vs 'new SMSNotification()' -- that's Factory's job.

Strategy designs the menu. Factory takes your order from it.

content.title: The Difference

content.value: Strategy = WHICH algorithm to RUN. Factory = WHICH object to CREATE. They're orthogonal -- Factory can create Strategy objects.

sections.title: Simple Factory: Move the if-else to One Place

content.value: The fix is dead simple: extract the creation logic into its own class. ONE class, ONE method, all creation decisions centralized.

content.title: Simple Factory

content.value: Add WhatsApp? ONE new case here. 10 services untouched. Constructor changes? ONE file to update. The enum gives compile-time safety -- no typo crashes.

content.question: Still 'new' keyword and tight coupling! The factory has 'new EmailNotification()' -- how is that decoupled?
content.answer:
Yes! Factory doesn't ELIMINATE coupling -- it CENTRALIZES it.

Before: 10 files know about EmailNotification.
After: 1 file knows.

Someone must always call 'new' -- the question is WHERE. Going from 10 places to 1 place is a massive win for maintenance, testing, and change management.

content.value: Simple Factory handles 80% of real-world cases. Don't over-engineer past this unless you have a specific reason.
Simple Factory has a switch statement. Every new type means modifying that switch. Can we eliminate even that?

content.title: Registry Factory

content.value: New type? One line: registry.put(TELEGRAM, TelegramNotification::new). Zero existing code modified. True Open/Closed Principle.
Registry stores RECIPES (Supplier/lambda), not finished dishes. Objects are created lazily -- only when supplier.get() is called.

sections.title: "But What About SMTP Config and API Keys?"

content.question: Products have different constructor parameters. EmailNotification needs SMTP host, SMSNotification needs an API key, PushNotification needs an FCM token. How does a factory with a single create(type) method handle this?
content.answer:
Great question. If the factory loads SMTP config for email, API keys for SMS, and FCM tokens for push -- all inside one method -- it becomes a God Factory. It knows everyone's private details, violating SRP.

When creation is trivial (just 'new X()'), Simple Factory is perfect. When creation involves private knowledge per type, we need something different...

content.title: God Factory Anti-Pattern

content.value: Simple Factory works beautifully when creation is trivial. But when each product type needs private setup knowledge -- SMTP hosts, API keys from vaults, device tokens -- stuffing it all into one factory creates a monster. Enter Factory Method...

sections.title: Factory Method: The Hook Inside the Service

content.value: Instead of a separate factory class, make creation an abstract METHOD inside a service class. Each subclass overrides this method with its own private creation logic.

content.title: Factory Method Pattern

content.question: Where is the factory CLASS? I just see a service with a method. Where did the factory go?
content.answer:
There IS no separate factory class in Factory Method. The 'factory' is a METHOD (createNotification) inside the service class. That's why it's called Factory METHOD, not Factory Class.

Each subclass overrides that one method to decide what to create. The factory lives as a method, not as a standalone class.

sections.title: This is Template Method + Creation Hook

content.value: Look at the abstract NotificationService again. The sendNotification() method defines a WORKFLOW: validate → create → send → log → audit. This is the Template Method pattern. The createNotification() call is the HOOK that subclasses fill in.
Without this abstract class, every service would duplicate the validate/log/audit logic. That duplication is the REAL reason Factory Method exists -- not the creation itself.

content.question: If creation logic is scattered across subclasses, isn't that WORSE than having it centralized in one factory?
content.answer:
It's justified ONLY when each piece of scattered logic is PRIVATE KNOWLEDGE belonging to that subclass.

EmailService knows SMTP config. SMSService knows Twilio API keys. UPIService knows NPCI endpoints.

If creation is just 'new X()' with no private knowledge, use Simple Factory. Don't scatter for no reason. Factory Method earns its place only through the combination of private creation knowledge + shared orchestration.

content.question: Factory Method mixes creation logic with business logic in the same class. Isn't that violating SRP?
content.answer:
It's intentional when creation and business logic are COUPLED BY DESIGN. UPI service needs UPI-specific creation (VPA validation, NPCI config) AND UPI-specific retry logic. They change together, so they belong together.

If creation and business logic are UNRELATED (OrderService creating Notifications), use a separate Factory class. The test: 'If creation logic changes, does business logic ALSO change?' YES = same class. NO = separate.

content.value: Factory Method earns its place through SHARED ORCHESTRATION around the product. No shared logic? Use Simple Factory. It's simpler and more readable.

content.question: The client code still says 'new EmailNotificationService()'. That's still hardcoded! How is this dynamic?
content.answer:
You're right. Factory Method alone doesn't solve client-side selection. It's an INTERNAL pattern -- it solves creation INSIDE the service.

For dynamic client-side selection you ALWAYS need something on top:
1. Simple Factory: ServiceFactory.create('email')
2. Registry/Map: services.get('email')
3. Spring DI: @Autowired Map<String, NotificationService>

content.title: Two Levels Working Together

content.value: Simple Factory picks WHICH service. Factory Method inside the service picks WHICH product. Two patterns, two levels, two different jobs.

sections.title: "India Has UPI AND Credit Card AND Visa..."

content.question: You showed India with just UPI. But India has credit card, debit card, Visa, AND UPI. Why tight-couple IndiaPaymentFactory with just UPI?
content.answer:
You caught a real design flaw! IndiaPaymentFactory returning ONLY UPIProcessor is wrong -- it forces India = UPI, which is false.

The fix: ask what TRULY varies together as a family.

Payment method (UPI, Credit Card) is NOT a family -- India uses both.
Compliance rules (GST tax + Indian receipt + INR currency) ARE a family -- they MUST be consistent per country.

So the Abstract Factory should create the COMPLIANCE family (tax + receipt + currency), and payment method should be a SEPARATE Simple Factory. Two axes of variation, two patterns.

content.value: This is the hardest part of Abstract Factory: correctly identifying what forms a true family. The test is: 'If I swap one product, MUST the others swap too?' GST tax REQUIRES Indian receipt format -- yes, they must swap together. But UPI does NOT require GST -- you can use Credit Card with GST too. So payment method is independent.
The Abstract Factory test: 'If I change one product in the group, MUST the others change too?' YES = true family (use Abstract Factory). NO = independent axes (use separate factories).

sections.title: Abstract Factory: Families That Vary Together

content.value: Here's the critical insight: identify what TRULY varies together as a family. Payment method (UPI, Credit Card) is NOT a family -- India uses BOTH. What IS a family: tax rules + receipt format + currency. These MUST be consistent per country.

content.title: Abstract Factory (Correct)

content.value: Abstract Factory = things that MUST vary together (tax + receipt + currency per country). Simple Factory = things that vary independently (payment method). Don't force independent axes into the same family -- that's false coupling.

sections.title: Spring DI: Where Did the Factory Go?

content.value: In production Spring applications, you rarely write manual factories. Why? Because Spring IS the factory.

content.title: Spring = Factory

content.question: Is that why I don't see abstract factory methods in my Spring codebase? Where did all the patterns go?
content.answer:
@Bean methods ARE factory methods. The @Configuration class IS a factory class. @Autowired Map IS a registry. The map.get() call IS the factory selection.

The CONCEPTS are alive and everywhere. The manual patterns aren't needed because Spring handles the wiring. Understanding Factory pattern helps you understand what Spring does under the hood -- and why the framework is designed the way it is.

content.value: Spring IS the factory. @Bean = factory method. @Autowired Map = registry. The concept lives on; the manual code doesn't.
Throughout this walkthrough, you kept asking: "Still new keyword. Still tight coupling." You were RIGHT every time. Factory doesn't eliminate coupling -- it MANAGES where it lives.

content.title: The Full Progression

content.value: Factory MANAGES coupling. It doesn't eliminate it. And that's the right goal -- centralize decisions so they're easy to find, change, and test.

sections.title: The Decision Tree: When to Use Which Flavor

content.value: After this entire walkthrough, here's your decision checklist:

content.title: Decision Flowchart
content.value:
How many types implementing the same interface?
  |
  +-- Only 1 stable type? --> Skip factory. Just 'new' it.
  |
  +-- 2+ types, chosen at runtime?
       |
       +-- Creation is simple (just 'new X()')?
       |    |
       |    +-- YES --> Simple Factory (80% of cases)
       |    |            Want zero if-else? --> Registry with Map<Type, Supplier>
       |    |
       |    +-- NO, creation needs private config per type?
       |         |
       |         +-- AND shared orchestration exists (log, retry, audit)?
       |              |
       |              +-- YES --> Factory Method (20% of cases)
       |              +-- NO shared logic --> Still use Simple Factory
       |
       +-- Products form consistent FAMILIES?
            (by country, theme, OS, platform)
            |
            +-- YES --> Abstract Factory
            |
            +-- Using Spring? --> @Bean + @Autowired replaces all of the above
Start simple. Upgrade only when you hit a real problem. Simple Factory → Registry → Factory Method → Abstract Factory. Never skip levels. YAGNI applies to patterns too.
